title: Data fusion and analysis of synthetic data in R
text: desire “fuse” otherwise integrate independent datasets long history, dating least early 1970’s (Ruggles Ruggles 1974; Alter 1974). Social scientists long recognized large amounts unconnected data “” – usually concerning characteristics households individuals (.e. microdata) – , ideally, like integrate analyze whole. aim falls general heading “Statistical Data Integration” (SDI) (Lewaa et al. 2021). prominent examples data fusion involved administrative record linkage. consists exact matching probabilistic linking independent datasets, using observable information like social security numbers, names, birth dates individuals. Record linkage gold standard can yield incredibly important insights high levels statistical confidence, evidenced pioneering work Raj Chetty colleagues. However, record linkage rarely feasible kinds microdata researchers use day--day (nevermind difficulty accessing administrative data). explosion online tracking social network data undoubtedly offer new lines analysis, time , least, social survey microdata remain indispensable. challenge promise recognized 50 years ago Nancy Richard Ruggles remains true today: Unfortunately, single microdata set contains different kinds information required problems economist wishes analyze. Different microdata sets contain different kinds information…great deal information collected sample basis. two samples involved probability individual appearing may small, exact matching impossible. methods combining types information contained two different samples one microdata set required. (Ruggles Ruggles 1974; 353-354) Practitioners regularly impute otherwise predict variable two one dataset another. Piecemeal, ad hoc data fusion common necessity quantitative research. Proper data fusion, hand, seeks systematically combine “two different samples one microdata set”. size nature samples involved intended analyses strongly influence choice data integration technique structure output. led relevant literature diverse convoluted, practitioners take different data “setups” objectives. context fusionACS, interested following problem: microdata two independent surveys, B, sample underlying population time period (e.g. occupied U.S. households nationwide 2018). specify “recipient” dataset B “donor”. goal generate new dataset, C, original survey responses plus realistic representation respondent might answered questionnaire survey B. , identify set common/shared variables X surveys solicit. attempt fuse set variables unique B – call Z, “fusion variables” – onto original microdata , conditional X.

Example: x = list(mean = c("v1", "v2"), median = "v3"). Target variables factors automatically return proportions ("mean") counts ("sum"). Target variables must exist implicates, static, created custom fun. implicates Data frame (data.table). Synthetic/fused microdata containing implicates, typically produced fuse. Implicates must row-stacked identified integer column named "M". static Data frame (data.table), optional. Static (non-synthetic) variables vary across implicates. Must satisfy nrow(static) == nrow(implicates) / max(implicates$M) match row ordering implicates. weight Character, optional. Name primary sampling weight column static. NULL (default), uniform weights equal 1 assumed. rep_weights Character vector, optional. Vector replicate weight column names static. provided, standard errors reflect additional sampling weight uncertainty across replicates. Character vector, optional. Column name(s) present implicates static defining population subgroups stratified estimation. Must return data.frame containing custom derived variables. var_scale Numeric. Scaling factor applied unadjusted replicate weight variance, determined survey design. text: data.table containing summary results grouped variables. Columns include: ... Subgroup groupings, specified. N Number observations per implicate analyzed subgroup. y Name analyzed target variable. level Factor level label (target variable y factor/logical). type Metric type: "mean", "proportion", "sum", "count", "median". est Pooled point estimate across implicates. moe Margin error corresponding 90% confidence interval.

title: Analyze Fused Microdata Implicates (Legacy) — analyze
text: Inputs checked consistent row dimensions implicate structures. Estimates standard errors computed independently implicate. final point estimate represents simple mean estimates across implicates (\(M\)). Standard errors degrees freedom pooled across implicates using Rubin's (1987) rules. replicate weights (rep_weights) provided, standard errors implicate account sampling design variance. Within-implicate variance evaluated around point estimate (equivalent setting mse = TRUE survey::svrepdesign). replicate weights absent, within-implicate variance means relies Cochran's (1977) ratio variance approximation (Gatz & Smith, 1995). Proportions use weighted variance formula, medians use asymptotic density approximation (large \(N\)) bootstrap sampling (small \(N\)).

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(fusionModel)  # Build a fusion model using RECS microdata fusion.vars <- c("electricity", "natural_gas", "aircon") predictor.vars <- names(recs)[2:12] fsn.path <- train(data = recs, y = fusion.vars, x = predictor.vars)  # Generate 30 implicates of the 'fusion.vars' using original RECS as the recipient sim <- fuse(data = recs, fsn = fsn.path, M = 30) head(sim)  # Full-sample analysis across multiple targets and metrics result <- analyze(   x = list(     mean = c("natural_gas", "aircon"),     median = "electricity",     sum = c("electricity", "aircon")   ),   implicates = sim,   weight = "weight" ) head(result)  # Mean electricity consumption by climate zone and urban/rural status result1 <- analyze(   x = list(mean = "electricity"),   implicates = sim,   static = recs,   weight = "weight",   by = c("climate", "urban_rural") )  # Subgroup analysis incorporating sample weight uncertainty via replicate weights result2 <- analyze(   x = list(mean = "electricity"),   implicates = sim,   static = recs,   weight = "weight",   rep_weights = paste0("rep_", 1:96),   by = c("climate", "urban_rural") )  # Custom derivation function prior to analysis my_fun <- function(data) {   kwh_per_ft2 <- data$electricity / data$square_feet   use_natural_gas <- data$natural_gas > 0   data.frame(kwh_per_ft2, use_natural_gas) }  result_custom <- analyze(   x = list(mean = c("kwh_per_ft2", "use_natural_gas", "electricity")),   implicates = sim,   static = recs,   weight = "weight",   fun = my_fun ) } # }

title: Internal LightGBM Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Fitting — fitLGB
text: Internal Fitting Routine LightGBM Models Workflow: Cross-Validation / Validation Evaluation: Loops candidate row hyper.grid. Depending whether cv.folds list fold indices logical flag, executes lgb.cv lgb.train early stopping enabled (early_stopping_rounds = 2L). Optimal Selection: Compares best_score across candidate hyperparameter sets, identifying set achieved lowest evaluation loss associated optimal number boosting iterations (best_iter). Final Model Training: Refits model full dataset (dfull) using optimal hyperparameters fixed iteration count (num_iterations = best_iter). Result Augmentation: Attaches metadata data.frame containing grid search results returned lgb.Booster object's $record_evals slot.

title: Impute Missing Data via Microdata Fusion — impute
text: impute() automates missing data imputation across complex microdata datasets following steps: 1. Sequential Ordering Variables containing missing values sorted total missingness imputed sequentially, starting variable fewest NA values. variable imputed, complete values immediately become available predictors subsequent target variables. 2. Predictor Screening larger datasets (>10,000 rows >20 columns), rank-based Spearman correlation matrix computed pre-screen predictors. maintain efficiency preserving strong predictor signals, candidate predictors target filtered meeting absolute correlation threshold (> 0.025), capped maximum 30 predictors (minimum 5). 3. Stratified Training Subsampling variables large non-missing sample sizes, stratified sample non-missing training observations (ranging 5,000 50,000 rows) selected speed model fitting. 4. Model Fitting & Fusion LightGBM fusion model trained using 80% validation splitting (nfolds = 0.8) determine optimal tree depth early stopping. Missing values target variable filled via fuse single implicate (M = 1). LightGBM natively accommodates NA values within predictor columns, partially complete predictor variables can used effectively model training. Parallel Processing Note: underlying computations LightGBM, fst, data.table rely OpenMP multithreading, recommended manage parallelism via cores parameter rather wrapping impute() inside outer forked parallel loops.

title: Impute Missing Data via Microdata Fusion — impute
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(fusionModel) # Load sample microdata and introduce random missing values (NA) data(recs) sample_data <- recs[, 2:7] set.seed(123) miss_mask <- replicate(ncol(sample_data), runif(nrow(sample_data)) < runif(1, 0.05, 0.25)) sample_data[miss_mask] <- NA  # Check missing value counts per column colSums(is.na(sample_data))  # Impute missing values imputed_data <- impute(sample_data)  # Verify that no NA values remain anyNA(imputed_data) head(imputed_data) } # }

title: Enforce Monotonic Relationships Between Numerical Variables — monotonic
text: monotonic() provides non-parametric post-processing rectify logical inconsistencies microdata fusion output (negative marginal prices non-monotonic tariff curves). Algorithmic Workflow: 1. Physical Sanity Checks expend = TRUE, boundary conditions enforced ensure zero consumption yields zero expenditure positive consumption yields positive expenditure. 2. High-Speed Subsampling length(x) > nmax, extreme boundaries (minimum maximum) preserved intermediate points randomly -sampled nmax efficient smoothing. 3. Super-Smoother Fit & Direction Detection initial non-parametric smooth fit via supsmu. overall correlation x predicted y determines whether relationship monotonic increasing decreasing. 4. Monotonic Coercion & SCAM Fallback Predicted values sorted enforce monotonicity. fast = FALSE sorting introduces substantial error (>5% relative divergence 5% points), constrained spline fit via scam. SCAM fitting fails converge, linear regression (lm) serves ultimate fallback. 5. Interpolation & Mean Preservation Fitted values mapped back complete original x vector using linear interpolation (approx). preserve = TRUE, resulting vector scaled weighted mean matches weighted.mean(y, w).

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(fusionModel) data(recs)  # Select candidate target (y) and predictor (x) variables y <- names(recs)[c(14:16, 20:22)] x <- names(recs)[2:13]  # Group first two y variables into a joint fusion block y_blocked <- c(list(y[1:2]), y[-c(1:2)])  # Run prepXY to determine preferred fusion ordering and predictor screening prep <- prepXY(data = recs, y = y_blocked, x = x, cor_thresh = 0.05, lasso_thresh = 0.95)  # Pass the prepared lists directly to train() trained_model <- train(data = recs, y = prep$y, x = prep$x) } # }

title: Train a Data Fusion Model — train
text: data data frame (data.table) containing donor microdata. Categorical variables formatted factors (ordered whenever applicable). y Character vector list. Variable(s) data fused recipient dataset. Variables modeled fused sequentially order provided. y list, element can character vector representing "block" variables sampled jointly fusion preserve multivariate dependence. x Character vector list. Predictor variable name(s) common donor recipient datasets. list, element specifies predictor set use corresponding element y. character vector, earlier y variables sequence automatically appended predictors subsequent y models. fsn Character string. NULL (default), uniform observation weights assumed. nfolds Numeric. Number cross-validation folds used LightGBM model tuning. 0 < nfolds < 1, represents proportion observations allocated training, remainder used validation (substantially faster full cross-validation). Default 5. nquantiles Numeric. Number quantile models fit continuous y variables addition conditional mean model. Specified quantiles evenly spaced. example, nquantiles = 2 (default) models 25th 75th percentiles. Even values recommended, conditional mean already captures central tendency. nclusters Numeric. Maximum number \(k\)-means clusters used group donor observations conditional expectation space. Higher values increase donor selection precision expense memory processing time. Set 0 Inf skip clustering (.e., treat every donor row cluster center). Default 2000. krange Numeric vector length 2. Specifies minimum maximum number nearest neighbors (\(k\)) evaluated selecting optimal candidate pools continuous conditional distributions. Default c(10, 500). hyper List. LightGBM hyperparameter grid custom values evaluate training. NULL (default), standardized baseline configuration optimized survey fusion used. See Details. fork Logical. TRUE, uses parallel processing via process forking (mclapply) across fusion steps. supported Unix/Linux/macOS platforms. Default FALSE. cores Integer. Number physical CPU cores allocated computation. fork = FALSE Windows, fusion steps processed serially, LightGBM utilizes cores internal OpenMP multithreading. fork = TRUE Unix, independent fusion steps executed concurrently across cores. title: Train a Data Fusion Model — train
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Load sample RECS survey dataset supplied with fusionModel data(recs) # Define fusion targets and common predictors fusion.vars <- c("electricity", "natural_gas", "aircon") predictor.vars <- names(recs)[2:12] # 1. Basic model training (saves output to working directory) fsn.path <- train(data = recs, y = fusion.vars, x = predictor.vars) # 2. Block fusion: Preserving joint dependency across component shares fusion.vars.block <- list(   "electricity",   "natural_gas",   c("heating_share", "cooling_share", "other_share") ) train(data = recs, y = fusion.vars.block, x = predictor.vars, fsn = "model_block.fsn") # 3. Custom predictor specification per fusion step xlist <- list(predictor.vars[1:4], predictor.vars[2:8], predictor.vars) train(data = recs, y = fusion.vars.block, x = xlist, fsn = "model_xlist.fsn") # 4. Hyperparameter override (Random Forest boosting alternative) train(data = recs, y = fusion.vars, x = predictor.vars,       hyper = list(boosting = "rf", feature_fraction = 0.6, max_depth = 10)) # 5. Grid search hyperparameter tuning train(data = recs, y = fusion.vars, x = predictor.vars,       hyper = list(max_depth = c(5, 10), feature_fraction = c(0.7, 0.9))) } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Build a fusion model using RECS microdata fusion.vars <- c("electricity", "natural_gas", "aircon") predictor.vars <- names(recs)[2:12] fsn.path <- train(data = recs,                   y = fusion.vars,                   x = predictor.vars,                   weight = "weight")  # Fuse back onto donor data (generating 20 implicates) sim <- fuse(data = recs,             fsn = fsn.path,             M = 20)  # Perform validation across population subsets valid <- validate(observed = recs,                   implicates = sim,                   subset_vars = c("income", "education", "race", "urban_rural"),                   weight = "weight")  # Inspect multi-variable summary diagnostics valid$plots$est valid$plots$moe  # Inspect single-variable diagnostic panel valid$plots$electricity$est  # Re-run plot generation separately to save PNG images to disk valid_saved <- plot_valid(valid,                           path = file.path(getwd(), "validation_plots"),                           width = 8, height = 6) } # }
